euclid.summary: Founder of systematic geometry who compiled the Elements, the most influential mathematical textbook ever written.
euclid.remarkableFact: The Elements remained the primary geometry textbook for over 2000 years, used continuously until the 20th century.

pythagoras.summary: Ancient mathematician and philosopher whose famous theorem about right triangles became foundational to mathematics and physics.
pythagoras.remarkableFact: Pythagoras believed numbers were the fundamental reality of the universe and that music, mathematics, and the cosmos were intimately connected.

archimedes.summary: Brilliant ancient mathematician and engineer who invented the method of exhaustion and made breakthrough discoveries in mechanics and hydrostatics.
archimedes.remarkableFact: Archimedes reportedly ran naked through the streets shouting Eureka! after discovering the principle of buoyancy while bathing.

eratosthenes.summary: Ancient mathematician and geographer who invented the sieve algorithm for finding prime numbers and accurately calculated Earth's circumference.
eratosthenes.remarkableFact: Eratosthenes calculated Earth's circumference to within 2% of the actual value using only geometry and observations of the sun's shadow.

apollonius.summary: Ancient mathematician whose treatise on conic sections was the most comprehensive treatment until the Renaissance.
apollonius.remarkableFact: Apollonius introduced the terms ellipse, parabola, and hyperbola for the three types of conic sections that are still used today.

diophantus.summary: Ancient Greek mathematician whose work on algebraic equations and integer solutions became foundational to number theory.
diophantus.remarkableFact: Diophantine equations, named after him, remain central to modern mathematics, with their solutions still puzzling mathematicians today.

al_khwarizmi.summary: Persian mathematician who wrote the foundational algebra textbook, from whose name the word algorithm derives.
al_khwarizmi.remarkableFact: The word algorithm comes from the Latinization of al-Khwarizmi's name, forever linking his identity to computational procedures.

fibonacci.summary: Medieval mathematician who introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to Europe and discovered the famous sequence now bearing his name.
fibonacci.remarkableFact: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in flower petals, spiral shells, and galaxy formations, making it one of mathematics' greatest discoveries.

cardano.summary: Renaissance mathematician who published the first systematic treatment of cubic and quartic equations, and wrote early work on probability.
cardano.remarkableFact: Cardano was a physician, philosopher, and gambler whose mathematical insights into games of chance laid groundwork for probability theory.

tartaglia.summary: Renaissance mathematician who solved the general cubic equation and contributed to ballistics and engineering mathematics.
tartaglia.remarkableFact: Tartaglia was largely self-taught, acquiring his mathematical knowledge without formal university education despite early trauma from a sword wound.

newton.summary: Revolutionary mathematician and physicist who invented calculus, formulated laws of motion and gravitation, and transformed science.
newton.remarkableFact: Newton did his most groundbreaking work during the Great Plague when Cambridge University closed, spending a year inventing calculus and discovering gravity.

leibniz.summary: Polymath mathematician and philosopher who independently invented calculus and made fundamental contributions to logic and symbolic notation.
leibniz.remarkableFact: Leibniz's notation for calculus (dy/dx, integral signs) proved so superior that it replaced Newton's, becoming standard in mathematics worldwide.

euler.summary: The most prolific mathematician in history, whose work touched virtually every branch of mathematics and physics.
euler.remarkableFact: Euler continued producing mathematics even after going blind, dictating to a scribe and averaging 800 pages of published work per year.

bernoulli_jacob.summary: Pioneer of probability theory and calculus whose Law of Large Numbers became fundamental to statistics.
bernoulli_jacob.remarkableFact: Jacob requested that a logarithmic spiral be carved on his tombstone with the inscription 'I shall arise the same, though changed.'

bernoulli_daniel.summary: Son of Johann Bernoulli who made major contributions to probability, fluid mechanics, and the theory of risk.
bernoulli_daniel.remarkableFact: Daniel Bernoulli solved the St. Petersburg paradox, demonstrating that expected utility differs from expected value in economics.

laplace.summary: Mathematician and astronomer whose work unified probability theory, statistics, and celestial mechanics.
laplace.remarkableFact: Laplace famously claimed that if we knew the position and momentum of all particles, we could predict the entire future, spawning determinism debates.

lagrange.summary: Versatile mathematician who made fundamental contributions to calculus, mechanics, algebra, and number theory.
lagrange.remarkableFact: Lagrange developed analytic mechanics, reformulating Newton's laws using calculus in a way that unified mechanics and provided powerful problem-solving techniques.

gauss.summary: Prince of mathematicians whose contributions span number theory, statistics, geometry, and physics with unmatched depth.
gauss.remarkableFact: As a child, Gauss instantly summed numbers 1 to 100 by recognizing a pattern, and his career maintained this level of genius.

fourier.summary: Mathematician and physicist who developed Fourier analysis and series to solve heat conduction problems, revolutionizing signal processing.
fourier.remarkableFact: Fourier analysis is ubiquitous in modern technology, from JPEG compression to WiFi, yet he developed it to understand heat diffusion.

bayes.summary: Presbyterian minister whose essay on probability introduced Bayes' theorem, fundamental to Bayesian inference and machine learning.
bayes.remarkableFact: Bayes' theorem was published posthumously and largely ignored until the 20th century, when it became essential to modern statistics and AI.

cauchy.summary: Prolific mathematician who formalized mathematical analysis, rigorous foundations for calculus, and complex analysis.
cauchy.remarkableFact: Cauchy was extraordinarily prolific, publishing nearly 800 papers and working in nearly every area of mathematics with rigorous proofs.

abel.summary: Brilliant but short-lived mathematician who proved the unsolvability of quintic equations and contributed to elliptic functions.
abel.remarkableFact: Abel died of tuberculosis at 26, yet left such profound work that the Abel Prize, mathematics' highest honor, is named after him.

galois.summary: Revolutionary mathematician who developed group theory and proved the unsolvability of polynomial equations by radicals.
galois.remarkableFact: Galois died in a duel at 20, supposedly over a woman, leaving behind mathematical work so revolutionary it took decades to understand.

riemann.summary: Profound mathematician whose ideas on geometry, analysis, and number theory shaped modern mathematics and Einstein's relativity.
riemann.remarkableFact: The Riemann Hypothesis, one of seven Millennium Prize Problems, remains unsolved and worth one million dollars, despite Riemann's suggestive insights.

cantor.summary: Mathematician who created set theory and revealed the hierarchy of infinities, revolutionizing the foundations of mathematics.
cantor.remarkableFact: Cantor's radical ideas about infinity were initially rejected and ridiculed; he suffered mental illness partly from the mathematical establishment's criticism.

dedekind.summary: Mathematician who developed set-theoretic foundations for number systems and created the notion of algebraic integers.
dedekind.remarkableFact: Dedekind's Cuts provided the first rigorous definition of real numbers, resolving centuries of uncertainty about the nature of continuous quantities.

weierstrass.summary: Rigorous mathematician who formalized real analysis, defined limits precisely, and discovered pathological functions challenging mathematical intuition.
weierstrass.remarkableFact: Weierstrass discovered a continuous function with no derivative anywhere, shocking mathematicians and showing that intuition can mislead.

bolzano.summary: Mathematician and philosopher who anticipated modern rigor in analysis, contributed to logic, and studied infinite sets.
bolzano.remarkableFact: Bolzano proved the Intermediate Value Theorem rigorously without using geometric intuition, exemplifying the revolution toward logical rigor.

boole.summary: Mathematician and logician who created Boolean algebra, the mathematical foundation of logic and digital computer design.
boole.remarkableFact: Boolean algebra was largely ignored until the 20th century, when it became essential to digital electronics and computing.

cayley.summary: Prolific mathematician who developed matrix algebra, advanced group theory, and contributed to non-Euclidean geometry.
cayley.remarkableFact: Cayley introduced matrices in 1858 and developed matrix algebra, yet matrices didn't become central to mathematics until the 20th century.

hamilton.summary: Brilliant mathematician who invented quaternions, revolutionized mechanics, and contributed to algebra and geometry.
hamilton.remarkableFact: Hamilton spent years obsessed with quaternions and carved the fundamental formula into a bridge in Dublin in a moment of inspiration.

lobachevsky.summary: Mathematician who developed non-Euclidean geometry, a hyperbolic geometry challenging Euclid's parallel postulate.
lobachevsky.remarkableFact: Lobachevsky's non-Euclidean geometry was initially rejected but later proven consistent, revolutionizing mathematics and enabling Einstein's relativity.

poincare.summary: Universal mathematician who made transformative contributions to topology, dynamical systems, and special relativity.
poincare.remarkableFact: Poincaré pioneered chaos theory by discovering sensitive dependence on initial conditions in three-body gravitational systems.

noether.summary: Revolutionary mathematician whose theorem connecting symmetries to conservation laws became central to theoretical physics.
noether.remarkableFact: Hilbert called Noether the greatest woman mathematician and her symmetry theorem unified mathematics and physics across quantum and classical domains.

klein.summary: Mathematician who unified geometry through group theory, developed the Klein bottle, and advanced non-Euclidean geometry.
klein.remarkableFact: The Klein bottle, a non-orientable surface with no boundary, was introduced by Klein and cannot exist in three dimensions without self-intersection.

hilbert.summary: Towering mathematician whose influential problems shaped 20th century mathematics and who championed formal logic and axiomatization.
hilbert.remarkableFact: Hilbert posed 23 unsolved problems in 1900, driving mathematical research for decades; some remain unsolved with millions in prize money.

peano.summary: Logician and mathematician who formalized arithmetic with axioms, contributed to symbolic logic, and studied space-filling curves.
peano.remarkableFact: Peano's space-filling curve, a continuous curve that passes through every point in a square, was initially thought impossible.

frege.summary: Philosopher and logician who founded modern logic and set theory, creating rigorous foundations for mathematics.
frege.remarkableFact: Frege discovered Russell's paradox just before publishing his opus, a devastating blow showing inconsistency in his logical system.

sylvester.summary: Brilliant algebraist who advanced matrix theory, invariant theory, and contributed to combinatorics and number theory.
sylvester.remarkableFact: Sylvester was expelled from Trinity College Cambridge for refusing to take religious oaths, yet became one of mathematics' greatest figures.

chebyshev.summary: Russian mathematician who advanced approximation theory, number theory, and probability through practical problem-solving.
chebyshev.remarkableFact: Chebyshev developed orthogonal polynomials to solve practical engineering problems, showing mathematics' power for applications.

markov.summary: Mathematician who developed Markov chains and advanced probability theory through analysis of dependent events.
markov.remarkableFact: Markov chains, invented by Markov, model everything from stock prices to DNA sequences and are foundational to machine learning.

lyapunov.summary: Mathematician who developed stability theory for dynamical systems and proved the Central Limit Theorem under weak conditions.
lyapunov.remarkableFact: Lyapunov stability, essential for control theory and engineering, determines whether systems return to equilibrium after perturbation.

godel.summary: Logician who proved fundamental incompleteness theorems, showing mathematical truth exceeds what can be formally proven.
godel.remarkableFact: Gödel's incompleteness theorems shattered Hilbert's dream of a complete axiomatic system, fundamentally limiting what mathematics can prove.

turing.summary: Logician and pioneering computer scientist who defined computability and developed the concept of the Turing machine.
turing.remarkableFact: Turing's contributions to the Enigma codebreaking effort saved countless lives; he founded computer science, then died tragically at 41.

von_neumann.summary: Brilliant mathematician who contributed to logic, set theory, quantum mechanics, game theory, and founded computer science.
von_neumann.remarkableFact: Von Neumann designed the modern stored-program computer architecture that all computers follow today, earning him computing's highest honor.

shannon.summary: Mathematical genius who founded information theory, showing how information can be quantified, transmitted, and protected.
shannon.remarkableFact: Shannon's master's thesis applied Boolean algebra to electrical circuits, creating digital logic; he also juggled, rode a unicycle, and played chess.

kolmogorov.summary: Prolific mathematician whose axiomatization of probability theory, dynamical systems, and complexity theory transformed mathematics.
kolmogorov.remarkableFact: Kolmogorov's axiomatization provided the rigorous foundation for probability theory, enabling its explosion as a mathematical discipline.

ramanujan.summary: Largely self-taught Indian genius whose intuitive discoveries in number theory and analysis anticipated modern mathematics.
ramanujan.remarkableFact: Ramanujan made thousands of discoveries with minimal training; mentors like Hardy called him the greatest mathematician of his era.

hardy.summary: Influential mathematician whose work on number theory and analysis advanced pure mathematics and mentored Ramanujan.
hardy.remarkableFact: Hardy wrote that mathematics is beautiful only when pursued without practical application, exemplifying pure mathematics philosophy.

erdos.summary: Prolific mathematician known for surprising results in discrete mathematics, graph theory, and number theory.
erdos.remarkableFact: Erdős lived nomadically, traveling constantly to collaborate and publishing over 1500 papers; mathematicians measure closeness by Erdős number.

mandelbrot.summary: Mathematician who discovered fractals and the Mandelbrot set, revolutionizing understanding of self-similarity in nature.
mandelbrot.remarkableFact: The Mandelbrot set, defined by a simple iteration, exhibits infinite complexity and is one of mathematics' most beautiful objects.

nash.summary: Mathematician who pioneered game theory and proved fundamental results about equilibrium, earning the Nobel Prize in Economics.
nash.remarkableFact: Nash equilibrium, central to economics and evolution, describes situations where no player benefits from changing strategy unilaterally.

wiener.summary: Mathematician who founded cybernetics and control theory, analyzing feedback systems in machines and living organisms.
wiener.remarkableFact: Wiener founded cybernetics, unifying control systems across machines and biological organisms through mathematics.

nyquist.summary: Engineer and mathematician who developed stability criteria and sampling theory fundamental to signal processing and control.
nyquist.remarkableFact: The Nyquist frequency determines the minimum sampling rate needed to accurately represent signals, enabling digital audio and data.

bode.summary: Engineer and mathematician who developed Bode plots and contributed to control theory and network analysis.
bode.remarkableFact: Bode plots, named after Bode, provide intuitive visualization of frequency response and are ubiquitous in engineering control systems.

dirac.summary: Theoretical physicist and mathematician who unified quantum mechanics and special relativity, predicting antimatter.
dirac.remarkableFact: Dirac's equation predicted the positron before it was discovered experimentally, one of physics' greatest triumphs.

heisenberg.summary: Physicist who formulated matrix mechanics and discovered the uncertainty principle, a cornerstone of quantum mechanics.
heisenberg.remarkableFact: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle shows that position and momentum cannot be simultaneously known with arbitrary precision.

schrodinger.summary: Physicist who developed wave mechanics and the Schrödinger equation, describing quantum systems' evolution.
schrodinger.remarkableFact: Schrödinger's wave equation is the fundamental equation of quantum mechanics, describing how quantum systems evolve in time.

boltzmann.summary: Physicist who founded statistical mechanics, connecting microscopic molecular behavior to macroscopic thermodynamics.
boltzmann.remarkableFact: Boltzmann's constant and his tombstone equation S=k log W remain central to physics, explaining entropy and time's arrow.

maxwell.summary: Brilliant physicist who unified electricity and magnetism into Maxwell's equations, revolutionizing physics.
maxwell.remarkableFact: Maxwell's equations showed that light is an electromagnetic wave, unifying optics with electromagnetism and predicting radio waves.

einstein.summary: Revolutionary physicist whose theories of relativity transformed our understanding of space, time, gravity, and energy.
einstein.remarkableFact: Einstein's E=mc² showed the equivalence of mass and energy, one of physics' most profound and practical insights.

planck.summary: Physicist who founded quantum mechanics by introducing energy quanta, revolutionizing physics at small scales.
planck.remarkableFact: Planck's constant determines the quantum-classical boundary and sets the scale of quantum effects throughout the universe.

lorenz.summary: Meteorologist and mathematician who discovered chaos theory and the butterfly effect in atmospheric modeling.
lorenz.remarkableFact: Lorenz discovered that tiny differences in initial conditions lead to vastly different outcomes, explaining why weather is unpredictable.

feynman.summary: Brilliant physicist who revolutionized quantum electrodynamics and influenced physics with unorthodox visualization techniques.
feynman.remarkableFact: Feynman developed intuitive diagrams for quantum processes and promoted learning through deep understanding rather than rote memorization.

black.summary: Mathematician and financial engineer who co-developed the Black-Scholes option pricing model, revolutionizing finance.
black.remarkableFact: The Black-Scholes formula transformed finance from art to science, enabling precise option pricing that became ubiquitous in markets.

scholes.summary: Mathematician who co-developed the Black-Scholes option pricing model with Fischer Black and Robert Merton.
scholes.remarkableFact: Scholes won the Nobel Prize in Economics for the Black-Scholes model, which became the standard for pricing derivatives.

merton.summary: Mathematician who extended the Black-Scholes model and developed stochastic calculus applications to finance.
merton.remarkableFact: Merton extended Black-Scholes to account for multiple assets and founded Long-Term Capital Management, illustrating both theory's power and limits.

wiles.summary: Mathematician who proved Fermat's Last Theorem in 1995, solving a problem unsolved for 358 years.
wiles.remarkableFact: Wiles spent seven years in secret proving Fermat's Last Theorem, finally solving mathematics' most famous unsolved problem.

perelman.summary: Reclusive mathematician who proved the Poincaré Conjecture, one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems.
perelman.remarkableFact: Perelman declined the Fields Medal and the Millennium Prize of one million dollars, preferring solitude over recognition.

tao.summary: Prodigious mathematician whose work spans number theory, harmonic analysis, and combinatorics, winning the Fields Medal.
tao.remarkableFact: Tao has made breakthrough contributions across multiple fields; he's known for his clear writing explaining advanced mathematics.

bengio.summary: Deep learning pioneer who contributed to backpropagation, convolutional networks, and attention mechanisms in artificial intelligence.
bengio.remarkableFact: Bengio won the Turing Award for contributions to deep learning, revolutionizing artificial intelligence and natural language processing.

hochreiter.summary: Machine learning researcher who invented LSTM networks, enabling deep learning with long-term dependencies.
hochreiter.remarkableFact: Long Short-Term Memory networks invented by Hochreiter enabled breakthrough applications in speech recognition and language translation.

page.summary: Computer scientist who co-founded Google and developed PageRank, a mathematical algorithm that revolutionized web search.
page.remarkableFact: PageRank, based on treating the web as a Markov chain, became the foundation of Google's search dominance.

brin.summary: Computer scientist who co-founded Google with Larry Page and developed PageRank algorithm with Page.
brin.remarkableFact: Brin and Page's PageRank algorithm outcompeted existing search engines by modeling web authority through mathematical graph theory.

dijkstra.summary: Computer scientist who developed Dijkstra's algorithm for finding shortest paths, fundamental to network routing.
dijkstra.remarkableFact: Dijkstra's algorithm efficiently finds the shortest path in networks and is used in GPS, routing, and countless applications.

knuth.summary: Computer scientist who wrote the definitive Art of Computer Programming and advanced analysis of algorithms.
knuth.remarkableFact: Knuth's Art of Computer Programming is considered the programmer's bible; he's known for being meticulous, even paying for errors found in his work.

vickrey.summary: Economist and mathematician who developed auction theory and mechanism design, revealing optimal bidding strategies.
vickrey.remarkableFact: Vickrey auctions ensure truth-telling through clever mechanism design and are now used in online advertising platforms.

myerson.summary: Economist who developed mechanism design theory, showing how to optimally design games and auctions.
myerson.remarkableFact: Myerson won the Nobel Prize for mechanism design, which underpins modern auction platforms like Google Ads and eBay.

nakamoto.summary: Pseudonymous creator of Bitcoin who invented blockchain technology, revolutionizing distributed systems and cryptocurrency.
nakamoto.remarkableFact: Nakamoto's identity remains unknown despite Bitcoin's success; their anonymity paradoxically demonstrates their creation's decentralization.

berners_lee.summary: Computer scientist who invented the World Wide Web, fundamentally changing how information is shared globally.
berners_lee.remarkableFact: Berners-Lee refused to patent the Web, allowing it to become free and open, arguably the most transformative decision in computing history.

pascal.summary: Mathematician and philosopher who contributed to probability theory, geometry, and developed mechanical calculation devices.
pascal.remarkableFact: Pascal's triangle and Pascal's principle appear across mathematics and physics, yet he abandoned mathematics for theology in his final years.

fermat.summary: Mathematician and lawyer whose work in number theory, optics, and calculus influenced modern mathematics despite publishing little.
fermat.remarkableFact: Fermat's Last Theorem, a handwritten note in a book margin, remained unsolved for 358 years until Andrew Wiles proved it in 1995.

descartes.summary: Philosopher and mathematician who invented coordinate geometry, linking algebra and geometry in revolutionary ways.
descartes.remarkableFact: Descartes developed coordinate geometry while lying in bed during illness, revolutionizing mathematics through algebraic representation of geometry.

newton_gregor.summary: Mathematician known for Newton's method and forward difference formulas, contributing to numerical analysis.
newton_gregor.remarkableFact: Newton's method for finding roots converges quadratically, making it one of the most powerful and widely used numerical algorithms.

brahmagupta.summary: Indian mathematician who introduced zero as a number and developed algebraic techniques in ancient mathematics.
brahmagupta.remarkableFact: Brahmagupta's work on zero was revolutionary; he treated zero as a number, not just a placeholder, enabling modern arithmetic.

bhaskara.summary: Medieval Indian mathematician who solved quadratic equations, studied infinite series, and made astronomical calculations.
bhaskara.remarkableFact: Bhaskara understood zero and negative numbers intuitively, solving problems centuries before European mathematicians grasped these concepts.

mobius.summary: Mathematician and astronomer who discovered the Möbius strip and made contributions to projective geometry.
mobius.remarkableFact: The Möbius strip, with only one side and one edge, is a non-orientable surface that confounds intuition and revolutionized topology.

clifford.summary: Mathematician who developed geometric algebra and contributed to non-Euclidean geometry before his early death.
clifford.remarkableFact: Clifford developed geometric algebra at 34, combining quaternions and exterior algebra into a unified framework.

hinton.summary: Cognitive scientist and machine learning pioneer who developed backpropagation and deep learning techniques.
hinton.remarkableFact: Hinton won the Turing Award for developing backpropagation, enabling the deep learning revolution that transformed AI.

lecun.summary: Computer scientist who invented convolutional neural networks, revolutionizing image recognition and deep learning.
lecun.remarkableFact: LeCun's convolutional networks enabled modern computer vision; they're used in facial recognition, autonomous vehicles, and medical imaging.

sutskever.summary: Machine learning researcher who developed sequence-to-sequence models and led deep learning research at OpenAI.
sutskever.remarkableFact: Sutskever's sequence-to-sequence models enabled neural machine translation, powering Google Translate and modern language models.
